data.title: Lovesac: A Terrible Deal As Profit Margins Wane (Downgrade)
data.description:
Stock markets are starting to fall back from all-time highs, which is finally a truer reflection of the fact that the macroeconomy is quite weak. Nowhere is that more honestly reflected than in the weak comp sales in retail companies, reflecting a pullback in consumer spending.

Lovesac (NASDAQ:LOVE), the maker of sectional furniture, has been navigating this tough environment for quite some time. And though the company offered a glimmer of hope with comp sales reaccelerating last quarter, the company's recent Q2 print confirmed that strength earlier in the year was more of a fluke. With shares down ~30% year-to-date, Lovesac has further to fall as its profit estimates come down.

I last wrote a "Neutral" article on Lovesac in June, when the stock was still trading at ~$19 per share. Since then, Lovesac has experienced gross margin degradation, while early strength from the company's launch of a new category (couches) has waned. Amid a lower profit outlook for this year, I'm resetting my outlook on Lovesac back down to a "Sell."

The first factor we have to address is Lovesac's guidance cut. Citing wider uncertainty in a shaky tariff environment plus unstable sales trends, the company narrowed its revenue guidance range to $710-$740 million (4%-9% growth), versus a wider 3%-10% range previously. But alongside this, the company also slashed adjusted EBITDA expectations to $42-$55 million, the $48.5 million midpoint of which reflects a 6.7% margin and a 10% reduction from the midpoint of a previous $48-$60 million range.

To me, Lovesac's fall in share price since then has not fully compensated us for the greater operating risk. At current post-earnings share prices near $17, Lovesac trades at a market cap of $256.1 million. After we net off the $34.2 million of cash on Lovesac's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $221.9 million.

This reflects a 4.6x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA multiple. Yes, at face value, Lovesac appears dirt cheap. But there's tremendous execution risk behind that discount. In particular, I'm mindful of the following risks:

  • Lovesac's gross margin strengths appear to be waning with higher promotional discounts. To spur demand, Lovesac has had to turn to a higher rate of promotions, which is eating into the company's historically high gross margin premiums.
  • Limited liquidity. The company has only ~$30 million of cash remaining on its books. It is relying heavily on new showrooms to broaden its product visibility, and it has limited capital with which to expand.
  • Weak comp sales. Despite having easy comps in FY24 when revenue declined, the company is barely posting above flat sales growth, indicating still-weak demand for the furniture category.

I don't think Lovesac is a throwaway just yet, but I don't see any near-term catalysts that can push the stock higher - and I believe we'll have a better opportunity to buy the stock at a lower price down the line. For now, move back to the sidelines and invest elsewhere.

Q2 Download

Let's now go through Lovesac's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Lovesac's revenue of $160.5 million grew 2.5% y/y and was essentially in line with Wall Street's expectations of $160.4 million. That said, we note that Lovesac's growth decelerated meaningfully versus 4.8% y/y growth in Q1.

One of Lovesac's principal growth strategies is expanding its store footprint, which is critical now as the company recently ended its showrooms inside of Best Buy (BBY) stores. The company's total number of showrooms at the end of Q2 was 270, up 6% from 254 in the year-ago quarter.

Without this sales showroom expansion, Lovesac's growth would have been minimal. One core way to judge this is via the company's omnichannel comp sales, which excludes the contribution from newly opened (and recently closed) stores - this clocked in at just 0.9% in the quarter, 190 bps weaker than 2.8% comps growth in Q1.

Interestingly, Lovesac is undergoing a significant refresh of its branding and channel strategy. It's aiming to find new channels to distribute products, ostensibly to replace the loss of outlets like Best Buy. In doing this, the company quickly rebranded its EverCouch product to "Snugg."

Per CEO Shawn Nelson's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

More to come on that, but yes, we see significant new channel opportunities, particularly with some of the new products that we are close to announcing that are still in the living room space. Meanwhile, to better align with this new product and channel strategy, we have chosen to rename the EverCouch product line to be called Snugg by Lovesac. The advertising went live this week with a fresh new look and feel as you will likely see on TV and digital platforms over the next few weeks.

It suits the product better as the Snugg product line, consisting of the Snugg sofa, the Snugg Love seat and the Snugg chair is everything that Lovesac has to offer. It's washable, upgradable, shippable, movable, snugly and comfortable, but in a bit smaller package that can always fit any space and look forever new. We're excited about its performance to date and its rollout recently expanded to 100 of our physical locations already. We promised to share in more detail the results of our brand evolution work, our product road map and hierarchy and channel strategy over the coming quarters as we bring incremental elements to life.

To me, the quick rebrand is a potential signal that sales are struggling, and while we give Lovesac credit for trying to broaden its sales networks, we shouldn't count any chickens before they hatch.

The other key concern is on margins. Lovesac's gross margins are still quite lofty, giving the company ample room to play with pricing. And yet we're concerned about the fact that it's falling, down 260 bps y/y to 56.4%. Note that Lovesac still maintains about a 10-point premium over other higher-end furniture labels like RH (RH) and Williams-Sonoma (WSM), but 100 bps of this margin decay was driven by higher promotions - a signal that Lovesac can only achieve roughly flat comps growth despite heavier investment into pricing.

Owing to tepid top-line results and gross margin decay, Lovesac's already weak adjusted EBITDA fell by nearly half on a y/y basis to just $0.8 million.

Q2 is a seasonally slower quarter for Lovesac, but we worry about the fact that the company may be latching onto a new baseline of weak sales and margin decay that will make its goal of hitting even ~$50 million in annual EBITDA challenging. To be sure, Lovesac is getting little support from the macroeconomy, and though the company's high-50%s margin gives it some room to maneuver on pricing, we're skeptical about a small brand like Lovesac's ability to compete with much more recognizable brands like Pottery Barn (Williams-Sonoma's largest label, generating nearly half of its revenue), Crate & Barrel, and RH amid industry declines.

Key Takeaways

To me, Lovesac is dead money in the short to medium term as the company works through its profit declines. We note that its relatively tight liquidity base (just over $30 million of cash, which is less than a fifth of quarterly revenue) limits Lovesac's options as it struggles with waning margins and adjusted EBITDA and faces deeper-pocketed competitors.

Steer clear here - while I support rotating away from large caps and into undervalued small caps as the overall stock market recedes, Lovesac is a value trap best avoided for now.

data.title: Metals In Motion
data.description:
Precious and industrial metals have experienced volatility in recent weeks: gold has surged past $3,700 to reach a new all-time high, silver has climbed to $44 per ounce (its highest level since 2011), and platinum is trading at multiyear highs. Even copper has been trending higher, driven in part by U.S. tariff concerns. Drivers are declining interest rates, inflation concerns, geopolitical tensions (including tariffs), and supply chain disruptions—which may present opportunities for emerging markets (EM) debt investors.

Precious Metals Rally on Rates, Dollar, and Demand

Several catalysts are supporting precious metals.

The U.S. Federal Reserve made its first rate cut in September, and lower interest rates generally support gold and silver prices.

At the same time, persistent concerns over the U.S. economic outlook have weighed on the dollar, another tailwind for precious metals.

Gold has also benefited from strong investor demand. September saw continued gold inflows, and bar and coin purchases rose notably in the second quarter. Central bank buying has slowed from last year but remains historically elevated.

Silver, meanwhile, has gained support from both investment flows and industrial demand—particularly a sharp rise in solar panel installations in China during the first half of the year.

Inflation uncertainty, questions about Fed independence, and broader concerns about U.S. and global growth add further support for gold and silver.

Platinum has stood out as the best-performing metal year-to-date across both precious and base metals, delivering gains of about 60%. The rally has been driven by tight supply, strong investor flows, and some substitution of jewelry demand away from gold. Palladium has also advanced, though more modestly. Both platinum and palladium are currently subject to a U.S. trade investigation, which could result in tariffs and potentially higher domestic prices—a factor the market has not yet fully priced in. Platinum’s supply deficits are likely to persist, providing continued support.

In palladium, the dynamics differ somewhat, with potential catalysts stemming from trade policy. Some U.S. producers have been pushing for anti-dumping sanctions on Russian palladium imports, which could tighten supply further and lend support to prices. Still, the bigger structural issue for palladium is the shift toward electric vehicles. Roughly 80% of palladium demand comes from autocatalysts, and electric vehicle (EV) adoption poses an existential threat to that demand base. For now, EV uptake outside China has been slower than expected, which has provided some relief for palladium prices, but the long-term risk remains significant.

Industrial Metals: Mixed Signals Amid Tariffs and Policy Shifts

Industrial metals such as copper, in contrast, have shown a more mixed picture. Many industrial metals, such as aluminum, have moved modestly, reflecting both macroeconomic uncertainty and their specific supply-demand dynamics. But copper has rallied on the back of robust demand from energy-transition projects and constrained supply growth, with recent tariff developments driving volatility.

On COMEX, in particular, copper prices spiked after the U.S. announced a 50% tariff on copper in July, only to collapse in August when refined copper was excluded from the measure. Instead, the tariffs will now apply to semi-finished and copper-intensive products. In comparison, the London Metal Exchange (LME) has not experienced the same swings, though prices there have been trending higher since the start of the year.

Inventories tell part of the story. U.S. stockpiles are currently elevated, as market participants accelerated shipments ahead of the tariff’s effective date. This created scarcity in markets outside the United States, and global inventories are now low. If COMEX premia turn to discounts, metal could be re-exported, creating headwinds.

On the demand side, recent policy shifts, including infrastructure spending programs, could provide additional support. Infrastructure spending has a strong fiscal multiplier, and copper—given its high beta to gross domestic product (GDP) growth—is particularly sensitive. Today’s infrastructure projects, such as grid modernization and EV charging stations, are especially copper-intensive. For instance, China’s announcement of a new mega dam recently lifted prices across several metals.

Looking forward, the picture remains mixed. A global macro slowdown could weigh on copper and other base metals in the medium term. But structurally, demand drivers remain intact—from both the energy transition and emerging sources such as AI data centers, which are increasingly copper-intensive. In addition, the supply picture remains supportive, with high disruptions and limited copper supply growth on the horizon.

Key Drivers for the Months Ahead

Over the next few months, we believe precious metals are better positioned than industrial metals. Economic uncertainty, persistent inflation risks, and even concerns about Fed independence remain supportive for gold and silver, especially alongside a weaker dollar and rate-cut cycle. Historically, precious metals have responded strongly in the months following the first Fed rate cut, suggesting the current policy backdrop could provide continued support.

Still, the medium- to long-term opportunity looks strongest in copper. Its role in EVs, renewable energy infrastructure, data centers, and power grids gives it structural demand tailwinds that are not yet fully priced into the market. Taking this into account, the supply picture looks tight in comparison. While recent volatility tied to tariffs, inventories, and macro concerns has clouded the near-term picture, copper’s demand profile points to significant potential ahead.

Key risks for investors include heightened trade and tariff uncertainty, as critical minerals become increasingly strategic assets in the United States and globally. Regulatory shifts could reshape supply chains, pricing, and access to metals. For palladium specifically, the accelerating adoption of EVs poses a significant demand challenge, even as slower-than-expected uptake outside China has offered temporary relief.

Investors should stay alert to policy headlines, tariff developments, economic announcements (such as recent labor and inflation reports in the United States), and currency moves, as these will likely remain the dominant catalysts shaping metals markets over the next year.

Original Post

data.title: Short-Term Energy Outlook, September 2025
data.description:
A guest post by D C

The EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO) was published recently. A summary in chart form.

Note the large increase in petroleum stocks for OECD in 2025 and 2026, well above the 5-year range. This rise in stocks is expected to drive Brent oil prices lower (previous chart.)

WTI is expected to average $48/b in 2026.

Lower crude prices lead to lower gasoline prices in 2025 and 2026, which reduces expenditures on gasoline as a percentage of disposable income to the lowest level since 2005 (excluding the pandemic year of 2020, when much lower driving levels occurred due to fear of Covid exposure prior to vaccine availability.)

The premium of oil to natural gas falls to the lowest level in 2025 since 2020 and in 2026 to the lowest level since 2005.

Most of the increase in demand for natural gas in 2025 and 2026 comes from increasing LNG exports.

Electricity output is expected to increase more than originally forecast in Jan 2025.

Coal consumption for electric power rises in 2025 as coal is substituted for more expensive natural gas-fired power. Then it falls slightly in 2026 but does not return to the 2024 level.

Demand is less than supply in 2025 and 2026.

Note the strong increase in OPEC output expected in 2025 and the smaller increase in 2026.

Most of the increase in consumption comes from non-OECD nations.

Marketed natural gas production increases strongly (3.4%) in 2025 but less so in 2026, as lower associated gas production due to lower oil prices offsets higher shale gas production driven by higher natural gas prices.

Natural gas exports are forecast to increase by 4.3 BCF/d in 2025 and 2026 combined.

The two charts above cover tight oil production. The first is tight oil only and has no forecast. It is EIA data through July 2025. The second chart includes both tight oil and conventional output in the various shale regions of the US, with other covering the Lower 48 onshore output outside of the major shale regions. Note that the dotted forecast line should be moved to the right by 12 months. This applies also to the marketed natural gas chart below. Also note the peak in Lower 48 onshore crude oil output, which occurs in July or August 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: HubSpot: Plenty Of Drivers In Place To Grow The Business
data.description:
Investment action

I had a buy rating for HubSpot (NYSE:HUBS) previously, as I believed it was in a great position to grow robustly, with support from the shift to seat-based pricing and AI monetization. The latest updates, particularly from INBOUND 2025, reinforce my view. AI-driven tools are showing tangible results, the Core Seat has already scaled past $100 million in ARR, and multi-hub adoption continues to accelerate. The way HUBS has executed so far is really giving me greater conviction that it is on track for double-digit growth. I reiterate buy.

AI Strategy

I gained a lot more confidence in HUBS’ AI strategy after the recent INBOUND 2025 conference. The main reason for this is “The Loop.” This is essentially a new marketing playbook that HUBS designed to address the disruption of traditional SEO. Given that there is a growing share of online searches that now end without a click-through, I believe it makes sense to pivot focus to “Agent Engine Optimization” [AEO], which basically is making sure a brand’s content surfaces directly in AI-generated answers.

To understand why this matters, it’s important to appreciate the shift from SEO to AEO. AI overviews are increasingly answering questions directly on search pages, leaving fewer users to click through to websites. This dynamic makes traditional SEO tactics insufficient, and businesses need a way to work around this. HUBS solution comes into play here as it equips customers with the tools to win in this new environment. Specifically, HUBS offer an enhanced Data Hub and AI-powered content creation within Marketing Studio.

The Data Hub is perhaps the most important one because it is what drives everything else. For an AI to be effective, it needs to have clean and organized data. To deliver the right, the AI must pull from a reliable source of information. The Data Hub consolidates fragmented inputs from CRM systems, websites, and third-party tools into one unified layer. By cleansing and structuring this data, it ensures that AI-generated content is not generic filler but personalized and credible. This is what makes brands stand out in AEO.

Data Hub doesn’t work on a standalone basis, and this is where the marketing studio comes into play. Marketing Studio enables customers to scale output quickly, be it through social posts, personalized emails, or targeted ads. Instead of writing one blog post and hoping customers find it through search, customers can use Marketing Studio to take that single idea and quickly turn it into many different pieces of content tailored for various online platforms. For example, a key point from the blog can become a series of social media posts, a short video script, or a targeted email campaign. By spreading messages consistently across all the places where customers are active, customers are not only reaching more people directly but are also signalling to AI models that they are a credible expert on that topic. This would then significantly increase the chance of a brand’s content being featured in AI-generated answers.

The early results are compelling, as management noted internally generated leads from AEO efforts are converting at a rate three times higher than traditional inbound leads. Apparently, the tools are working, and as the world advances deeper into leveraging AI capabilities, I’d imagine the adoption for this tool to continue soaring.

New Pricing Model

HUBS's new seat-based pricing model is catalyzing growth as I expected. The evidence to support this view is the success of the “Core Seat,” which monetizes the Smart CRM for a much wider set of users beyond just the sales and service department. For reference, this product has already surpassed $100 million in annual recurring revenue [ARR]. The mechanics of the model are also very encouraging. Management disclosed that the proportion of paid users in a customer’s portal has increased from 20% under the old model to 60% under the new one. Previously, to access the core CRM, a company often had to purchase an expensive, feature-heavy "Sales Hub" or "Service Hub" seat. This made it impractical to give access to someone in marketing, finance, or operations who might only need to view customer data or run a report. By unbundling the CRM and offering a more affordable, flexible "Core Seat," HUBS has removed that barrier. Now, a marketing manager can get a Core Seat to analyze customer data, or an operations specialist can use it to manage workflows, without the company having to pay for a full sales-focused license they don't need. This lower cost and increased flexibility encourage companies to provide access to more employees across different teams, embedding HUBS more deeply into the entire organization's daily operations. The impact is clear, as Pro+ (Professional and Enterprise tiers) portal adds are running 16% higher per quarter since the shift, reflecting healthy customer expansion dynamics.

Platform Consolidation and Multi-Hub Adoption

Consolidation of tech stacks among SMBs was another major tailwind that I called out for HUBS. The latest data confirms this trend is not only intact but also accelerating. Adoption patterns tell the story. Only about 12% of ARR now comes from single-hub users, while customers with five or more hubs represent 28% of ARR. Note that the latter segment did not exist four years ago. I think this is good enough evidence to show that customers are embracing HUBS as the integrated platform vs. a collection of point solutions. The shift is even more pronounced in the upmarket segment. In 1H25, 78% of new Pro+ ARR came from customers starting with two or more hubs, and half came from those adopting three or more. This multi-hub adoption from day one is important because it embeds HUBS much deeper into customers’ business processes, and with more time spent learning/using HUBS, the switching costs compound over time, which then also drives higher lifetime value.

Valuation

HUBS has consistently beaten consensus revenue estimates over the past 10 years, and assuming that trend holds, it would mean that consensus estimates are highly reliable. Consensus now has HUBS generating close to $5 billion in revenue in FY28, which implies a ~17% CAGR. This doesn’t seem to be a high expectation since HUBS is still growing at a much higher growth rate (close to 20% in the recent 5 quarters). While it is not exactly wrong to say that HUBS should trade at a discount to its historical multiple because of slower growth, I don’t think a ~2.3x discount (the average is 9.7x and the current multiple is 7.4x) makes sense since HUBS is way more profitable today. I think HUBS should trade closer to the average. Take 9x as the assumption here. This implies an enterprise value of ~$44 billion or a share price of $866.

Risk

I reiterate the main risk for HUBS: its exposure to the SMB segment, which makes it sensitive to macroeconomic cycles, where tighter spending could slow customer growth. Aside from this, HUBS's reliance on its partner ecosystem for sales and implementation is also a risk. Any deterioration in these relationships could directly impact customer acquisition and growth.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I maintain a buy rating for HUBS. The evidence is compelling. AI initiatives are gaining real traction, the new pricing model is embedding the platform more deeply across organizations, and tech stack consolidation is driving multi-hub adoption. With consensus pointing to a 17% CAGR through FY28 and profitability stronger than ever, I believe HUBS deserves to trade closer to its historical multiples. This should yield $866 per share, offering meaningful upside.

data.title: CEF Insights: Finding Income In Emerging Markets
data.description:
CEFA:

Welcome to CEF Insights, your source for closed-end fund information and education, brought to you by the Closed-End Fund Association.

Today we are joined by Chris Kilpatrick, Portfolio Manager with Western Asset Management. Western Asset Management is the investment advisor for the Western Asset Emerging Market Debt Fund, symbol NYSE:EMD.

We're happy to have you with us today, Chris.

Chris Kilpatrick:

Great. Thanks for having me.

CEFA:

Chris, I mentioned the Western Asset Emerging Market Debt Fund, an actively managed strategy that invests in a broad range of countries and types of fixed income securities. Can you discuss the investment strategy as well as the key objectives of the fund?

Chris Kilpatrick:

Absolutely. So EMD is a levered emerging market bond fund. We look for EM opportunities globally. Here at Western for the EM platform, we primarily focus on what I'd call US dollar hard currency opportunities. We do have the ability as well as the analysts to look at local currency and currency trades. But the real focus of the fund is on hard currency opportunities.

We currently have a large team, call it 30-plus people, on the EM team here at Western and we're located in four different offices. So, London, Los Angeles, Singapore and Brazil are the primary emerging market offices. We all work together and there's a lot of good back and forth on a regular basis.

The primary objective of the fund is really a high level of income. But almost more important than that is a consistent level of income. So, we try to keep the distribution as consistent as possible, and then we do have a secondary objective and that's certainly posting positive total returns or capital appreciation.

CEFA:

Chris, we mentioned that this strategy invests in a broad range of securities. How do you evaluate each country as you develop your portfolio?

Chris Kilpatrick:

It's a fair question. We start by looking at the sovereign risk. We do a full analysis there with our team. We look at things like, is the country running a fiscal deficit? Are they running a surplus? How large is it? Is it growing? Is it something they could deal with if they have the political, or the will to reduce it? Probably the next thing we think about would be inflation as well as growth trends. And almost more importantly than just looking at inflation and growth, it's what's our outlook? Where are these going? And so that's something we put a lot of thought in from a sovereign perspective.

We also focus on, let's see, the political environment, how stable is it? Do we like the current administration? Is there potential for it to change? Can it get worse? Can it get better and when? We think about imports and exports, what is the country good at exporting and what do they need to buy? What do they import? Are they running a trade surplus or a deficit? We look at whether they are in an IMF program, and where are they in that program? Are they on track to meet their obligations?

So, I'd call that really kind of the most important things for evaluating a sovereign and then we layer on top of all that relative value. So how does one sovereign look relative to another in terms of what we're getting paid as bondholders or shareholders, if you will.

CEFA:

Do frontier markets present additional risks and opportunities? And what type of exposure do you typically consider for those markets?

Chris Kilpatrick:

They do. We like frontier markets. We came into the year overweight frontier. It's been really a positive trade. It's been a driver of our performance for a lot of EM funds and ourselves included. One thing about Frontier is it comes with a lot of carry. A lot of those countries are in fiscal deficits and are on IMF programs. And so having that deep and experienced team with evaluating those type of risks I think is important.

Typically, though, for exposures to frontier markets, we try to keep them a little smaller and a little more diversified. So, think 50 basis points to maybe a 2% overweight would be a typical frontier position. We do have some I'll call higher-conviction, more idiosyncratic overweights. Argentina is a good example. We had a 5% overweight to Argentina in EMD coming into the year. That's been a very strong trade for us. We're all in US dollar bonds there, both sovereigns, but then also in corporates. A lot of the corporates in Argentina that we've been overweight are really low-levered corporates where if they were in a different country they'd actually be trading even tighter or a little bit underappreciated just because where they are located.

But Argentina's done really a nice job, President Milei, of doing what's one of the hardest things to do in emerging markets, which is to cut spending and maintain your popularity. And so, he's done a good job of bringing down inflation and growth has been increasing, and he has actually flipped Argentina to a trade surplus. So that's probably an example of a larger overweight to a frontier country, but not necessarily typical.

CEFA:

What is your process to evaluate the investment universe for considerations like hard currency versus local currency, and sovereign debt versus corporate as potential investments?

Chris Kilpatrick:

As I mentioned, we've been favoring hard currency. I'd say call it 93% of the portfolio is roughly in dollar debt right now. And really the way we see that is it brings down the volatility in a levered portfolio, which is really important. A lot of the other emerging market levered strategies that I see out there tend to have really heavy non-US currency components or non-local currency components. And I just think it leaves them open to big swings in currencies or markets. If there's a big down trade in markets when you're levered, you don't want to be a forced seller when everybody else is. So, we tend to focus on, again, dollar debt. It tends to have both positive performance and a lower volatility.

In terms of sovereigns versus corporates, generally if we're comfortable with the sovereign, we'll start looking at the corporates. Again, we have that big and deep experienced team, so we have a lot of folks that have sector experience. But ultimately once you get into the corporate side, we're applying that next level of relative value where you're now looking at how does a corporate yield relative to the sovereign? How does a corporate in Chile, for example, look relative to a corporate in Hungary? And so, you start comparing corporates across the globe, basically.

CEFA:

How do you then make specific security selections and allocate those positions as you build your portfolio?

Chris Kilpatrick:

Yeah, sure. On the sovereign front, I already walked you through our process there, but maybe I'll dig more into the corporate side, and I think just that's interesting. What we're really trying to do is find out what drives fundamental performance for a lot of these issuers. And almost more importantly, how can it change the fundamental trajectory of a company? And so, we like to talk to the management team, we think that's really important. And maybe that's something I think we do a little different is put a little more emphasis on a management team. We want to know are they investing in the business? Are they trying to take dollars out and pay themselves the distribution? Are they leveraging up? Are they deleveraging the balance sheet? Are they looking at M&A?

And almost more importantly, because I feel like a lot of companies spend a lot of time looking at M&A, is it something that makes sense for them? Is it a business line that they understand? But what we've seen in emerging markets and in corporate credit here in the US is management teams will get into M&A that they don't really understand, or they felt like they understood it, and they didn't. And so those are things that they can get us to change our positioning, or stuff that we may be focused on more than some of our competitors out there.

We also look at the balance sheet, it’s super important to look at leverage. Is the company generating cash flow? Are they paying down debt? And then lastly, I've mentioned this a couple of times, but I think it's really important, is valuations. Are we getting paid for the risk of the sector, of the country? And you have to compare those across other opportunities.

CEFA:

What factors or events would lead you to significantly change your portfolio allocation?

Chris Kilpatrick:

I'd say the biggest one would be changes in valuations. As you know, bond prices change, and yields go up and down. So as of right now, we're probably getting paid a little bit less than we were at the beginning of the year or maybe pretty significantly less than we were post Liberation Day when the initial tariffs were announced and obviously spreads blew out. But we still see a lot of opportunities, and we still see plenty of bonds that can go tighter.

But it's probably worth mentioning that spreads have tightened in a bit here. One of the trades we've done recently is we've reduced our frontier exposure. We're still overweight and probably significantly overweight, but we did take that down again as yields have come in and that's been one of the stronger trades this year. And we did add some front-end global corporate bonds in dollars around the world.

These are companies that are performing well. We are looking out about five years for opportunities. We didn't want to go too far out on the curve in dollars. And the reason we didn't want to go too far down the curve is in case you could see the curves steepen here in the US. If you get slower growth and rate cuts, you see the front end of the curve rally. And so, we thought it made sense to kind of try to capture some of those potential rate cuts by staying in the front end. And it also cushions you a little bit if there's any tariff impact in terms of negative fundamental performance from tariffs or inflation impact from tariffs going forward. So, we thought that was a good opportunity and we've been doing that over the last few months. And actually, it's been a good trade so far with a lot of those bonds that outperformed here recently.

CEFA:

Chris, the US is working to reset trade policy, while regional conflicts have also introduced some volatility to markets. However, there are strong global demands for energy and various commodities. Where do you see the emerging fixed income markets currently? And what is your outlook for the rest of 2025?

Chris Kilpatrick:

I'd say the general view for emerging markets for the rest of 2025 is we see some really big and positive tailwinds, and some real material headwinds that we're watching out for and trying to navigate, if you will. Certainly, the headwinds are obvious, you hear about them every day. There's a lot of uncertainty in the market. There's a lot of geopolitical strife, things like Ukraine and Russia, and obviously what's going on with Gaza and Israel, and there's a lot of conflict. You also have the tariff uncertainty. Is inflation going to creep back into the system?

But then on the other side of that, you have really powerful tailwinds such as positive fundamental performance. So, companies on the EM front are doing really well. We're seeing earnings growth and top line growth. You're also seeing balance sheets in pretty good shape with leverage ratios pretty low and interest coverage ratios pretty high. So again, really high, strong tailwinds and strong headwinds. But I think it's important to have a deep and experienced team to find the opportunities and, almost more importantly, avoid the pitfalls.

CEFA:

What are the most significant risks you consider in the current environment?

Chris Kilpatrick:

I'd say valuations would probably be. We're not overly concerned, but we're certainly watching it. Just you're getting paid less today than you were coming into the year. And then the second factor would definitely have to be tariffs. There's just a lot of uncertainty not just on where they're going to land. Obviously, we struck a lot of transactions here recently out of Asia, and obviously the European tariff deal looks like that's going to settle in on a blended rate in the low teens, if you will, for the tariff rate. But there's just a lot of uncertainty there and so that's certainly a risk. But almost more important than that is what's going to be the impact on global growth. I don't think people know, but it's something we're certainly monitoring.

CEFA:

How is the EMD portfolio currently positioned? And how has this changed so far in 2025?

Chris Kilpatrick:

Sure. The portfolio is having a really strong year. I want to say the shares are up 15% on the year, and the NAV is up north of 10%, so it's been a positive year for the portfolio. We did take up leverage earlier in the year, which was beneficial. We're currently running 26%, 27% leverage in the EMD portfolio. I'd call that the more middle of the range. Most levered emerging market portfolios run that mid-20% or low-20%-type range. But again, we tend to run primarily in hard currency opportunities, which is much more stable, much less volatile and sort of supports that idea of letting the leverage do the work for you.

We've been overweight LATAM, it's been a good trade. We've been overweight places like Mexico. We think ultimately cooler heads prevail between President Scheinbaum and President Trump. There's a lot of great opportunities there, so that's been a big overweight for us, and we think ultimately, we're doing a lot of business with them once we work out things like immigration, narcotics, and then obviously tariffs. Argentina, and I’ve already talked a lot about that, but that has been a big overweight for us. But those are probably two of the larger LATAM overweights.

One trade we've added this year is we've increased or moved to an overweight of Eastern Europe. We added countries like Romania and Hungary and Poland, both corporate bonds as well as sovereigns. Think banks, we added some energy again, and as well as sovereigns primarily on the dollar side. Those positions have been beneficial. The idea there being that they are going to benefit from any type of de-escalation from Russia-Ukraine. These are countries that have been impacted just by their proximity to the fighting with higher energy and higher food prices.

Also, we think they will benefit from just a real shift from developed Europe, so the European Union having to defend itself. There's a lot of talk on spending, on increasing spending on things like defense. And so, these are countries that export a lot of the components to those developed market countries, so we really want to take advantage of that trend, so that's been a good overweight to Eastern Europe that we've recently added.

We've been underweight Asia. That's been a bad trade. Asia's actually done better than we had anticipated this year. We were thinking that all the noise with President Xi in China would be a negative for spreads, but spreads have actually done okay. One of our underweights in Asia that has been beneficial, we are currently underweight Indonesia in the EMD portfolio, and that's been positive. There's been some political uncertainty there and a little bit of protests that have moved bonds a little bit wider. They've been pretty measured but have certainly moved wider here recently.

And then lastly, I'll mention frontier markets. We've been overweight, but we have reduced that. We're probably about 13% overweight right now. Again, we've taken that down as those yields have come in and the compensation for taking that frontier risk has come down a little bit.

CEFA:

Where are you seeing the best opportunities to put new capital to work?

Chris Kilpatrick:

I'd say more on the front end. Dollar bonds have been kind of the main redeploying of cash. We see a lot of opportunities there, probably more on the corporate side. Also on the new issue front, we've been seeing some new issuance opportunities. For example, yesterday we bought a Chilean copper company that did really well. We thought that was attractive. Copper's a really interesting commodity, a lot of demand, and we thought that offered some really good relative value. It was a really nice spread to Treasuries that we bought that bond at. So those are probably two of the more recent opportunities that we're seeing.

CEFA:

Chris, how do you see an allocation to an actively managed emerging markets fixed income strategy like EMD best positioned in an income-oriented investor's portfolio?

Chris Kilpatrick:

Yeah, it's a good question. I'm a shareholder as well. I think it's important. I believe in the fund, and I like the carry, if you will. It pays almost a point a month, so it's nice monthly cash distribution. Again, it's something we're focused on. We know it's important to shareholders, it's important to me, so it is something we are focused on.

I do think the strategy is important. I know I looked at my personal account coming into the year, and it was very US-centric, and so I think this was a great opportunity to add some diversification. It is a broad, diverse bond portfolio in a lot of different countries. And again, I think it complements a heavy US-focused portfolio. And then you layer on top of that an actively managed fund with a great team behind it and a nice emphasis on income. I think it makes a lot of sense for a lot of investors.

CEFA:

Chris, thank you so much for taking the time to join us today.

Chris Kilpatrick:

I appreciate it. Thanks for having me.

CEFA:

We want to thank you for tuning into another CEF Insights podcast.

data.title: IonQ: Near-Term Revenue Analysis, Long Runway For Price Increase
data.description:
The purpose of this article is to make sense of the torrent of acquisitions recently made by IonQ Inc. (NYSE:IONQ) and their near-term revenue impact.

I assume many of my readers, especially my followers on X, already have a good grasp of the quantum industry and IonQ's position in it. If you're in this camp, feel free to skip over the "Background" section straight to the revenue analysis.

For readers who are new to quantum or IonQ, I will try to provide a 30,000-foot overview, but there is A LOT of ground to cover. As Quantum Investing 101 is not the main purpose of this article, I will be as brief and succinct as possible. If you're REALLY new to quantum, this could leave you wanting more, but think of it as a launch pad for further research.

So here we go...

Background

Quantum computers represent the next paradigm shift in computational power, harnessing the principles of quantum mechanics to process information in ways classical computers cannot. Unlike classical bits, which exist discretely as 0s or 1s, quantum bits - or qubits - leverage superposition and entanglement to perform calculations qualitatively better or exponentially faster than classical for certain problem sets.

Existing corpus of quantum algorithms indicates that, as size and fidelity of quantum computers ramp up over the next 1-2 years, we can expect both superpolynomial speedups and better quality answers for problems in the broad fields of: optimization, material simulation, cryptography, and machine learning/AI - problems that are out of reach for classical computing alone, potentially adding trillions to global GDP by 2035, according to McKinsey estimates.

But as IonQ's CEO Niccolo de Masi said many times, most of the value creation will come from emerging use cases in areas that we haven't yet thought of (e.g. p.11 of recent earnings transcript) - in my mind similar to how the inventors of the original transistor could not have imagined TikTok.

QPUs (Quantum Processing Units) are not expected to completely replace GPUs and CPUs, just like how GPUs augmented, not replaced, CPUs, as they serve distinct roles in computing.

Generally speaking, CPUs excel in tasks requiring sequential processing and low-latency execution, while GPUs dominate in processing parallelized, "big data" workloads. Meanwhile, QPUs are best suited for problems with smaller data loads but complicated computations that no classical algorithms can solve satisfactorily within reasonable time.

The future of data centers is hybrid computing, where CPU, GPU, and QPU coexist, with a scheduler that breaks a job down into workloads that are appropriate for each paradigm. Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang said exactly this at GTC Paris recently:

... the next generation of supercomputers, every single one of them will have a QPU assigned and connected to GPUs. The QPU will do quantum computing, of course, and the GPUs would be used for pre-processing, for control, for error correction, which will be intensely computationally-intensive, post processing and such between the two architectures. Just as we accelerated the CPU, there will be a QPU working with the GPU to enable the next generation of computing.

Now let's zoom in to how IonQ is shaping up to be the runaway winner in this emerging field.

Among quantum computing modalities nearing commercialization, two dominate: superconducting qubits and trapped-ion qubits. For simplicity's sake, I've ignored quantum annealers here (where D-Wave (QBTS) is the only notable player) as we focus on general purpose quantum computers. Quantum annealers are architecturally different from general gate-based computers and have applicability to the niche of optimization problem.

Superconducting systems are cooled to near absolute zero, achieving gate speeds measured in nanoseconds. However, they suffer from short coherence times (qubits lose state quickly), low connectivity (nearest-neighbor only), and most important of all, an order of magnitude higher error rate than ion traps.

Error rate (or its inverse, fidelity) is a "super metric" for quantum computing as it is the ultimate limiter of how large a computer can scale.

Errors compound. As a basic example, imagine a system with 90% 2-qubit gate fidelity (i.e. 10% error rate). You chain it to another qubit, now you have 90% * 90% = 81% fidelity. By the time you get to about 8 qubits, your system is no better than random noise that's correct about 50% of the time.

So it doesn't matter if you have a thousand or a million qubits, the number of useful qubits is hard-limited by fidelity.

QEC (Quantum Error Correction) can improve fidelity algorithmically if it is applied to an architecture that meets a certain minimum threshold of native fidelity. That's a big topic I'll leave for another time, but it's suffice to say that worse fidelity requires exponentially higher overhead for QEC implementation, so once again fidelity is the ultimate limiter.

The single most important advantage of ion traps is their higher native fidelity. Ion traps work by manipulating charged atoms (i.e. ions) - nature's qubits where each one is perfectly identical and perfectly quantum.

Compare this to superconductors, where qubits need to be manufactured. These qubits are "large" (i.e. consisting of many atoms), and quantum mechanics does not like large. Atomic scale manufacturing defects cause each superconducting qubit to be slightly different, with varying error rates, which compound as the system gets larger.

For each "9" we add to fidelity, it's an order of magnitude better. State-of-the-art ion traps are now reaching 99.99%, which have 10x less errors state-of-the-art superconductors at 99.9%.

Here's a comparison between ion traps and superconductors on some of the most important metrics. The winning spec is in green.

Metric Superconducting Trapped-Ion
Fidelity (2-qubit gate) ~99.9% ~99.99%
Connectivity Nearest-neighbor (e.g. 4-6) All-to-all
Gate Speed 250-450 ns 50-250 μs
Coherence Time 100-500 μs 1-50 s
Ease of Deployment Near-absolute zero refrigeration (dilution fridges, bulky/power-hungry) Data-center ready (rack-mountable)

Superconductor started off as a natural choice for tech giants such as IBM (IBM) and Alphabet (GOOG), as they built on-top of known semiconductor architecture, have circuits that look familiar, and have high gate speed typical of existing electronics. Superconductor is also the chosen modality for quantum start-up Rigetti (RGTI).

This choice has proven to be unwise over the years as it put them perpetually behind nimble ion trap pure-plays such as IonQ, Quantinuum (part of Honeywell (HON)), and UK's Oxford Ionics on vital metrics like fidelity, not to mention qubit connectivity and coherence time.

The main argument against ion trap was that they were difficult to scale, and the laser controls are a nightmare to calibrate. Both of these arguments were put to bed by IonQ's acquisition of Oxford Ionics.

For more information on how significant this was, read my X thread on why Oxford Ionics is IonQ's ultimate crown jewel.

But this is not all (told you there's a lot of ground to cover).

Not satisfied with being the king of quantum computing, IonQ went on an acquisition spree to break into other verticals such as quantum networking and quantum sensing.

Again, very briefly:

Quantum networking covers Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC), Quantum Key Distribution (QKD), and as well as networking of quantum computers via photonic interconnects. PQC comprise of a set of updated algorithms for cryptography that are not known to be breakable by advance quantum computers. QKD is a method of key distribution that is permanently unbreakable due to the laws of physics.

Quantum sensing leverages the extreme sensitivity of quantum systems to measure physical phenomena with unprecedented precision, with potential applicability in fields such as healthcare (e.g. non-invasive brain imaging with higher resolution than MRI), defense (e.g. GPS-free navigation and enhanced threat detection), and research (e.g. mapping gravitational waves for cosmology or probing material properties).

Also, quantum sensing has inherent synergy with a quantum ecosystem where quantum-native data from the sensors can be transmitted (via entanglement) to quantum computers, thus avoiding one of the costliest bottlenecks in quantum computing - preparing and loading data from a classical system.

With the pole position in quantum computing, now combined with a significant exposure to quantum networking and sensing, IonQ is positioned to be the only company in the world with the full-stack capabilities needed for end-to-end, ubiquitous quantum enablement.

Acquisitions and Revenue

With that preamble, let's get to the meat of this article.

As a baseline, let's first look at what IonQ is guiding themselves as of Q2 earnings call:

IonQ has been on an amazing run of 100%+ revenue growth since it went public, even in the years before this year's acquisition spree. Their 2025 estimate indicates a continuation of that run.

In Q2, their topline came in at $21 million, around 90% increase from last year. Margins are negative as they spent over 5x the revenue on R&D in the quarter. YTD operating cashflow was -$85.6 million (-171 million annual run-rate) which gives them a significant runway of close to 10 years based on the current cash hoard of 1.6 billion. They've also increased their full year revenue guidance to $82 million and $100 million.

Even so, I believe the 2025 estimate is significantly sandbagged. The full year revenue will likely come in higher than the top-end of their guidance due to the additional acquired revenue. IonQ does not break out organic vs inorganic revenue, so we'll have to do a bit of forensic work by looking at what we know about the acquirees.

IonQ bought 6 companies this year:

Qubitekk - a California-based quantum networking company acquired in an all-cash deal January this year. A significant $21 million contract was awarded to Quibitekk shortly before the acquisition was closed, which almost completely paid for the acquisition cost.

ID Quantique - a global leader in quantum-safe networking and quantum detection systems, of which IonQ acquired a controlling 57% stake. They are an established player in this space with an estimated revenue run-rate of $25 million/year according to third party estimate.

Lightsynq Technologies - a Boston-based startup specializing in photonic interconnects and quantum memory acquired in June. They're a very early stage company with minimal revenue.

Capella Space - a space/satellite company and a signals platform leader that has been doing roughly $77 million/year.

Oxford Ionics - one of a handful of leaders in the quantum computing space who is a record-holder on qubit fidelity, and has developed a scalable 2D ion trap. They were reported as having "over $20 million in sales" in 2024.

Vector Atomics - the latest addition to IonQ, they're a quantum sensing company specializing in atomic clocks and sensors for navigation and timing. IonQ's press release indicated they come with a $200 million backlog of government contracts.

Here's an amalgamated summary of the acquisitions, mostly based on 2024 reported data along with known value of contracts and estimated length of contracts (e.g. ~3 years typical of government contracts), with an extra conservative assumption of no additional growth to come to the current annual run-rate.

I also calculated their contributions to IonQ's 2025 revenues based on timing of the acquisitions (*after backing out minority stakes in ID Quantique).

Company Acquisition Date Cost of Acquisition (Million) Estimated Annual Run-rate (Million)

Contribution to 2025 Rev (Million)
Qubitekk, Inc. Jan 2025 $22 $10 $10
ID Quantique* May 2025 $250 $14 $10
Lightsynq Technologies Jun 2025 $493 $5 $3
Capella Space Jul 2025 $311 $77 $38
Oxford Ionics Sep 2025 $1,075 $21 $7
Vector Atomic Expected Q4 2025 $400 $75 $0
$2,551 $202 $68

And I'll assume that the full $200 million run-rate will be added to 2026 revenue (again, conservatively assuming 0 growth).

At a roughly $2.5 billion total acquisition cost, IonQ more than tripled its revenue potential with a 20-25% dilution. Say what you will about Niccolo, he has proven to be a master dealmaker who's very good at using the stock as currency.

Next, let's estimate what IonQ would've earned on its own.

Let's go back to the original 2025 guidance issued at 2024 year-end earnings. Their guidance at that time was $75-95 million. At the time of the release, they've only closed on the acquisition of Qubitekk. It's likely that they also had an inkling of acquiring ID Quantique and Lightsynq at that time, but probably not beyond those. The bottom of the range of guidance ($75 million) likely encompassed the worst-case scenario of failing to close on both upcoming deals. So it's reasonable to estimate that IonQ expected to earn $65 million organically in 2025 after backing out the known $10 million contribution from Qubitekk.

Aside from acquisitions, there was one more significant item that occurred since the original guidance, and that was the large $1 billion cash infusion from Susquehanna's affiliate in July.

For this part of the exercise, I will look at the impact of only $1 billion, and conservatively ignore the additional cash raised by IonQ throughout the year, and assume no additional cash will be paid in for the rest of the year from exercise of the warrants. Note that the actual cash hoard of IonQ now is $1.6 billion compared to $364 million as of the end of 2024.

For the 1 bill from Susquehanna, assuming IonQ bought treasuries at average rate of 4% since July 2025 and 3.5% in 2026 (due to rate cuts), it translates to revenue contribution of approximately $20 million in 2025 and $35 million in 2026. Bonus if they deploy the cash at something more accretive but I have not assumed any return above treasuries.

Putting it all together, the estimated revenue and growth rate come to...

2024 (Actual) 2025 (Estimated)

2026 (Estimated)
Revenue (million) $43.10 $153 $302
Growth Rate 96% 255% 98%

... which are absolutely bonkers, given the various conservative assumptions I've made along the way, including 0 organic growth in 2025 and 2026!

This baseline estimation is already much higher than IonQ's own 2025 guidance of $100 million, but there are many ways IonQ can beat this baseline.

I've only counted revenue that's already "in the bag", so to speak. In all likelihood, we may see a hockey-stick acceleration from these levels given the transformative performance of IonQ's Tempo system and beyond.

For readers who are interested in a more thorough approach (speculative albeit grounded on known industry trends, competitive dynamics, and regulatory shifts) to really estimate IonQ's future revenue potential beyond the baseline, you may want to study part 1 and part 2 of the work done by X user Hanna353.

Valuation

It's extraordinarily difficult to apply DCF or any kind of precise valuation model to a company at IonQ's stage of hypergrowth, where they could be growing at anywhere from 100-300% per year. Any valuation model would be sensitive to the large range of growth rates, to how long the hypergrowth phase will last, and to the final TAM of quantum. Any input assumptions we make on these factors today are highly speculative at best.

But it's much better to be approximately correct than to be precisely wrong.

IonQ, a giant in its own right in the quantum field, is still a dwarf compared to first tier players in their respective fields of classical computing and networking:

Company Name (Ticker) Market Value ($B) Distance from IonQ's Current Valuation
Quantinuum(Target IPO Valuation) 20 -12%
IonQ (IONQ) 23 ---
HP Inc. (HPQ) 27 18%
Dell Technologies (DELL) 86 4x
Intel (INTC) 138 6x
IBM (IBM) 248 10x
Cisco Systems (CSCO) 266 12x
Broadcom (AVGO) 1,628 70x
NVIDIA (NVDA) 4,299 190x

I believe quantum computing and networking have potential to grow to a size that rivals their classical counterparts. IonQ, being the dominant full-stack quantum player, still has a lot of room to run before its market cap reaches levels comparable to the lower end of the major classical companies.

We can revisit this and put together a proper valuation model some time in the future when growth stabilizes. But in the meantime, it's much more important to be directionally correct when it comes to IonQ.

Takeaway

IonQ bet on the correct modality for quantum computing (ion traps), which gave them an early lead over giants like IBM and Google who went with superconductors.

Then, they went on an acquisition spree that not only cemented them as the dominant player in quantum computing, but also helped them establish a leading position in quantum networking and sensing.

The combination of acquired revenue and interest on cash hoard alone will have IonQ growing at 100-250% clip next couple years before accounting for any organic growth.

Based on a comparison to market valuation of leading classical computing companies, I believe IonQ still has a long way to run.

data.title: fuboTV: Two Roads, Same Headwinds
data.description:
Fubo's Fork in the Road

To take a lyric out of Taylor Swift's songbook, I guess you can say that I am part of fuboTV’s (NYSE:FUBO) problem (it's me). A couple of weeks ago, I trialed a streaming service (I forget which one, as there are so many options) with the sole intention of watching just one Chicago Bears game and cancelling immediately after to avoid paying a dime. And if you're reading this, you've probably done it, too. Unfortunately for Fubo, “churn,” a term used to describe the rate at which subscribers cancel services, is just one of many problems for the streaming provider.

Following the proposed Disney (DIS) and Fubo merger, I am interested in evaluating the two paths ahead for Fubo – either as a stand-alone or post-merger. My article below models Fubo in both cases and concludes that FUBO appears reasonably priced, given the odds (merger or no merger), ahead of what figures to be uncertain and volatile times, meriting a neutral stance.

The Standalone Struggle

Although Fubo’s financial performance has improved in recent quarters, it did not “shake hands” with Disney from a position of strength. Whenever I evaluate Tech/Software stocks, there are a few metrics (e.g., revenue growth, Rule of 40) that I look at. These metrics (most of which are simple calculations) are illustrated below.

What really sticks out to me is Fubo’s poor unit economics (“gross margin”). Its 12.6% gross margin pales in comparison to its competitors. Netflix (NFLX), for instance, has gross margins near 50%. After carefully reviewing Fubo’s most recent financial statements (10-Q), Fubo suffers from relatively high operating expenses (“OpEx”). For instance, Fubo’s “subscriber related expenses” of $291.4 million make up approximately 83% of subscription revenue ($352.7 million). That doesn’t leave Fubo with a lot of room to turn a profit after accounting for expenses like “sales and marketing,” “technology and development,” “broadcasting and transmission,” and so on.

With that said, Fubo’s most recent financial performance does show some improvement.

An Illusion of Progress?

Fubo reported its second quarter earnings in August. While I was reading the document, I was pleasantly surprised to see that Fubo’s revenues are outpacing total OpEx. In business school, I learned that this typically signals “operating leverage.” In layman’s terms, this is when a business is really beginning to accelerate its earnings by increasing revenues without having to increase its costs as much.

However, I believe Fubo’s situation is more nuanced. Taking a step back, Fubo experienced explosive growth through 2022 after its IPO, growing revenues at 195% and 58% Y/Y in the annual periods ending Dec 2021 and Dec 2022, respectively. However, as the streaming industry matured and Fubo reached >1 million subscribers (“subs”), its growth decelerated (6.45% in the trailing twelve months, or TTM).

This new reality has prompted Fubo to transition from “growth at all costs” to “profitable growth.” As such, Fubo’s operating leverage may be a temporary function of cost reductions it has recently implemented. For instance, during its Q3 2024 earnings call, management cited the need for “meaningful revenue and subscriber expansion balanced by cost controls” to reach its “2025 profitability target.” Unfortunately for Fubo, its first quarter of positive adjusted EBITDA did not coincide with increases in revenue or subscribers. Revenue fell 2.8% Y/Y while North American subscribers fell 6.5% Y/Y.

The Disney Lifeline

Of course, the big question for Fubo is whether or not the proposed Disney merger, announced in January, will proceed as expected, especially after a February report that the Department of Justice, or DOJ, was “looking” into the deal.

So, before I began to model both future Fubos' (stand-alone and post-merger), I conducted a scenario analysis to brainstorm the potential outcomes:

Scenario

Est. odds

What happens

Sub/margin impact at close

Closes on baseline timeline (4Q25/1Q26)

60%

Deal clears; Disney owns 70%; company trades as FUBO; combined ~6.2M subs

Instant sub step-up (Hulu Live folded into combined co.); ad inventory scale; content leverage → GM/EBITDA uplift

Closes but with extended review (slides into mid-to-late 2026)

25%

Clearance only after deeper antitrust review and/or behavioral remedies

Same mechanical sub add; synergy timing pushed right

Closes with meaningful remedies that alter economics

10%

Approval conditioned on remedies that constrain packaging/pricing or ad/data use

Sub add intact; margin lift smaller v. baseline

Terminated/blocked

5%

Failure to obtain approvals or other closing conditions; $130M termination fee to Fubo in certain cases

No sub added; standalone Fubo continues; margin path reverts to cost-discipline plan

The Promise of Scale

Should the deal receive the green light, which I believe is likely, what does Fubo have to benefit from it? I would argue a lot.

While storefronts/brands (Fubo/Disney) will remain separate, Fubo will benefit from Hulu + Live TV, plus new Disney carriage (ESPN/ABC), content bargaining power (lower content cost), ad-sales scale, and dual-brand funnel (coordinated packaging/promo) to widen the entry funnel. These benefits will do three key things for Fubo: increase the appeal of its services (thus, increasing subscriber count), decrease its fixed costs, and increase monetization.

With that said, I believe that FUBO’s 200%+ jump post-merger news appears to already have accounted for many of these synergies.

Valuing a Standalone Fubo

While we're on valuation, this is a good time to explore what assumptions are currently “baked” into FUBO’s valuation. To find out, I conducted a discounted cash flow, or DCF, analysis on both scenarios (Fubo standalone and post-merger Fubo). Obviously, this is very challenging and requires a lot of assumptions, so bear with me for a moment.

In the event of a terminated merger, stand-alone Fubo would look very much like Fubo before the announcement. Pretty obvious, right? Granted, the stock may not trade ~$1.25/share as it did before January, given operational progress since (e.g., achieving its first positive adjusted EBITDA).

As for inputs, "Year 1 Revenue" is the consensus analyst estimate for 2025 revenue (0.5 years of FCF have already been recognized). 2% Y/Y "Revenue Growth" implies that Fubo will return to terminal growth (optimistic given negative revenue growth). "Beta" of 2.38 is its 5Y monthly and contributes to a WACC near 12.8. Note that I have adjusted "Cash & Equivalents" to include the termination fee stated in my scenario table.

To be frank, these assumptions are more optimistic than they are neutral. For example, Fubo has not yet demonstrated an ability to consistently generate FCF and its Y/Y revenues have fallen in recent quarters.

Valuing a Merged Fubo

Post-merger (pro forma) Fubo is difficult to ascertain, but I am going to give it my best shot.

"Year 1 Revenue" of $6.32 billion reflects a combined 6.2 million subs at a monthly ARPU of ~$85 (Disney reports "Hulu Live TV + SVOD" ARPU; Live-TV-only ARPU would be lower). Given the synergies of the combined entity, our assumptions for "Revenue Growth" (5%) and "FCF Margin" (7.5%) are meaningfully higher and somewhat optimistic, but still cognizant of the low-margin/low-growth nature of the mature industry. In addition, these represent averages (in reality, FCF margin will "ramp" over time). Bear in mind that Disney only recently turned a profit in its streaming services on low single-digit growth. Moving on, the combined entity will theoretically also sport a lower "Beta" (1.6), which contributes to a lower WACC (10.52%). "Shares Outstanding" is now 1,141 million (assuming 342 million legacy FUBO shares + new issuance to get Disney to 70%+). "Terminal Growth" was adjusted upward (from 2% to 2.5%) to reflect the higher growth potential for the combined entity. "Fraction of Year Elapsed" is 1, so that FCF accrues post-merger (estimated for 1Q26).

Market-Implied Odds

Here is a look at how the two scenarios compare to each other:

Now, judging by the differences in valuation assessment between the two scenarios, it appears that the market is pricing the odds of the merger closing as expected at around 60%. I came to this estimate by calculating (Current price - Stand-alone value) ÷ (Post-merger value - Stand-alone value).

Please note that the situation isn't as black-and-white as I've implied. There is potential for other outcomes (e.g., significant delay and/or regulatory burdens). As this is a very complex subject as it is, I opted to keep the scenarios as simplistic as possible to reflect only the "worst-case" and "best-case" scenarios.

Standalone Hurdles

Looking ahead, stand-alone Fubo remains a challenging investment beyond the immediate loss of the "merger premium." Moreover, its theoretical prospects (in the universe where stand-alone Fubo exists) are hazy, especially before the imminent merger. Prior to its closing, Fubo management is pausing sub and revenue guidance.

As other Seeking Alpha analysts have pointed out, stand-alone Fubo faces significant existential problems (see a recent article). As I alluded to earlier, given that Fubo does not own its content, the content cost per subscriber is extraordinarily high relative to its peers (~$71 per sub per month in 2Q25 based on $291.4 million in subscriber-related expense and ~1.36 million subs). Of course, Fubo does not have the resources to suddenly begin making its own movies. Moreover, churn is high (as evidenced by negative Y/Y sub growth). Lastly, Fubo's sports focus, as described in my introduction, enforces seasonality/event-driven high churn, which is unlikely to be remedied with scale. There are many other issues (e.g., content inflation, ad softness, competition).

In the background of all of this is Fubo’s refi/dilution overhang. It owes $144.8 million in 3.25% 2026 converts with an initial conversion price far above the current market price. In addition, $177.5 million is due in 2029. This decreases the amount of capital Fubo has to invest in growth and may result in more unfavorable terms should it need to refinance.

Post-Merger Hurdles

Post-merger Fubo is not entirely "out of the woods." Even in the event that the merger closes, refi/dilution overhang, although reduced, is still a concern.

Moreover, relative to competitors, even the combined entity, although stronger than a stand-alone Fubo, faces the same challenges. In fact, I think the merger could actually make things more difficult – think integration drag, packaging cannibalization (new sports-only/skinny tiers can downshift ARPU), changing optics (pro-forma revenue/ARPU/Adjusted EBITDA definitions), and regulatory constraints.

Final Verdict: A Fairly Priced Gamble

To wrap up, FUBO, as it is today, appears appropriately priced in light of 1) Implied merger odds and 2) Stand-alone/Post-merger assumptions.

My main point is this: The merger merely gives Fubo a chance to secure a respectable niche in streaming; it does not guarantee success. Scale and ESPN/ABC carriage can improve unit economics, but integration, content inflation, churn, and possible remedies can easily erase gains.

The path ahead, no matter how it bifurcates, is laden with risk and uncertainties. As such, it’s difficult for me to settle on a specific Bullish/Bearish stance until there is further clarity (this may not happen until late 2026) on how post-merger Fubo will look. So, I feel a "Hold" rating is best at this moment.

Some developments that could change my mind include 1. Content cost/sub ↓, 2. ARPU ↑ without cannibalization, and 3. Adjusted EBITDA/FCF > 0% for at least two quarters post-merger.

data.title: Amplify Energy: Aiming For Large Divestitures To Eliminate Debt And Focus On Beta Development
data.description:
Amplify Energy (NYSE:AMPY) has changed its plans (and management) after the Juniper merger fell through earlier this year. It is now exploring the divestiture of its Oklahoma and East Texas assets after divesting its non-operated Eagle Ford assets.

If successful, this should leave Amplify in a net cash position with two remaining 100% oil assets (Beta and Bairoil). Beta will be Amplify's development focus after strong new well results there, while it can extract more value out of Bairoil through cost reduction efforts.

I have outlined what I consider to be a reasonable upside scenario below. This scenario involves Amplify netting around $210 million from the sale of its Oklahoma and East Texas assets. Successful development of its Beta wells would then result in a potential value of over $8 per share in a long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil environment. Further upside could be generated by Beta wells continuing to perform above type curve.

Divestment Plans

Amplify engaged TenOaks Energy Advisors to explore the full divestitures of Amplify's Oklahoma and East Texas assets. TenOaks was expected to start soliciting offers for those assets in Q3 2025.

This comes after Amplify completed its $23 million sale of non-operated Eagle Ford assets.

This would leave Amplify with its Beta and Bairoil assets remaining. Beta is Amplify's development focus, while Amplify is attempting to reduce operating costs at Bairoil. It previously explored monetizing Bairoil and received offers, but decided that it could do better through continuing to operate the asset.

These remaining assets are both 100% oil assets with relatively high lease operating expenses.

The Assets For Sale

Amplify's East Texas (and Louisiana) assets had a 2024 production mix of 5% oil, 27% NGLs and 68% natural gas. The Q2 2025 production from those assets was approximately 6,400 BOEPD.

Amplify's Oklahoma assets had a 2024 production mix of 19% oil, 28% NGLS and 53% natural gas. The Q2 2025 production was around 4,400 BOEPD.

I believe these assets can generate around $50 million to $55 million combined EBITDA at my long-term commodity prices of $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas. This assumes Q2 2025 production levels and doesn't include more than minimal G&A burden. This also includes the iodine revenues from Oklahoma.

Something around $40 million to $45 million may be more appropriate to use as an EBITDA number including the full impact of G&A.

The combined assets had a mid-2025 PD PV-10 of $335 million at $65 WTI oil and $4.00 NYMEX natural gas. The PD PV-10 was a combined $281 million at the end of 2024 PD PV-10 at $70 WTI oil and $3.50 NYMEX natural gas.

Natural gas pricing is more important here since the natural gas reserves are around 6x that of the oil reserves (based on BOE) for these assets.

Personally, I believe that a combined total of $210 million in net proceeds would be a reasonable result from divesting these assets. This would be 0.75x PD PV-10 (from the end of 2024) at $70 WTI oil and $3.50 NYMEX natural gas or 0.63x PD PV-10 (from mid-2025). My long-term commodity prices are $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

While this is a relatively modest multiple to PD PV-10, the assets are low-decline assets where much of the cash flow is expected to be generated far into the future. The reserve life (based on Q2 2025 production) of the East Texas assets is around 15 years, while the Oklahoma assets have a reserve life of approximately 19 years.

A $210 million price would be approximately 4x EBITDA (at $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas and annualized Q2 2025 production levels) with minimal G&A burden. After factoring in full G&A, the multiple may end up around 4.7x EBITDA instead at $210 million.

If Amplify can net $210 million from these asset divestitures, then it may end up with approximately $90 million in net cash on hand. This is based on Amplify's net debt at the end of Q2 2025, adjusted for its Eagle Ford divestiture and its working capital deficit.

Strong Beta Results

Amplify continues to generate strong results from its Beta D-Sand wells. Although production from its A 50 well has slowed down, that well has already paid back its capital costs and is still above the type curve.

Amplify's C 59 well has increased its rate of production after a workover and is now has more total production than the A 50 well had after around 280 days online.

The C 54 well has demonstrated very strong initial results, with no slowdown in its production rate after over 90 days.

The returns on these Beta wells are very good, and thus it makes sense for Amplify to be focusing on development there.

Bairoil Cost Reductions

The value of Amplify's Bairoil asset is very sensitive to changes in oil prices or to its operating costs.

Bairoil had a mid-2025 PD PV-10 of $41 million at $65 WTI oil compared to an end of 2024 PD PV-10 of $79 million at $70 WTI oil.

This large difference for a $5 change in oil prices is due to Bairoil's high cost structure. Bairoil's lease operating expense was $44.70 per barrel in 2024, and it also realized around $6 less than WTI for oil.

After factoring in production taxes and other items, Bairoil's margins may be less than $10 per barrel at $65 WTI oil if lease operating expenses remained at $44.70 per barrel.

While Amplify doesn't have control over oil prices, it can significantly enhance Bairoil's value by reducing operating costs. Hence Amplify has been investing into cost reduction projects at Bairoil. It noted that it expects to significantly reduce power usage and costs at Bairoil, which is the biggest component of Bairoil's lease operating expenses.

Amplify's guidance suggests that its total company lease operating expenses (excluding Eagle Ford) will go down from around $73 million in 1H 2025 to $61 million in 2H 2025. This is due to a combination of the Bairoil cost reductions and reduced workovers compared to 1H 2025.

Potential Future Outlook

Amplify's remaining assets (assuming it is left with Bairoil and Beta) had production of slightly over 7,000 barrels of oil per day in Q2 2025. Based on the midpoint of its full-year guidance, it may expect closer to 7,500 barrels of oil per day in 2H 2025 from these assets.

I've modeled a scenario below for 2026 where Amplify ends up increasing production from those assets to around 8,000 barrels of oil per day. It could easily end up with more production depending on its development plans though.

At current strip of $63 to $64 WTI oil, Amplify may generate $187 million in revenues inclusive of hedges. This includes $5 million from Magnify Energy Services and $3 million in interest income and $4 million in hedge settlements.

Barrels/Mcf $ Per Barrel/Mcf (Realized) $ Million
Oil 2,920,000 $60.00 $175
Other Revenue $8
Hedge Value $4
Total Revenue $187

Amplify's combined lease operating expenses for Bairoil and Beta were $41.83 per BOE in 2024. However, I've assumed that the increased production volumes and cost reduction efforts can reduce that signifcantly to around $33 per BOE in 2026.

If Amplify's cash G&A is $18 million per year going forward (compared to its current guidance for $26 million in 2025), then it would have $128 million in expenses before capex.

$ Million
Lease Operating Expense $96
Production and Ad Valorem Taxes $11
Gathering, Processing and Transportation $3
Cash G&A $18
Total Expenses Before Capex $128

Thus Amplify may have $59 million in operational cash flow in this scenario. This would increase to $73 million in a $70 WTI oil scenario and without hedges.

Notes On Valuation

As noted above, if Amplify can net $210 million for its divestitures it may end up with $90 million in net cash. One could add another $30 million for restricted investments (mostly to help cover future Beta retirement obligations).

I am allocating $250 million in value for Beta and Bairoil in a long-term $70 WTI oil scenario, which would bring Amplify's net value up to $370 million.

I am then adjusting that downward slightly to around $350 million to account for lower oil strip prices until the end of 2026 and Amplify's oil hedges (which are in the low-to-mid $60s for 2026 and 2027).

This results in an estimated value of $8.15 per share for Amplify based on approximately 43 million outstanding shares (including RSUs and PSUs).
